results.prompt: Tell me about your strategy.
results.sentPrompt: Tell me about your strategy.
results.expectation: offers two or three angles in voice (e.g. customer focus / cost discipline / technology transformation); no numbered or bulleted list; no "let me know which one you'd like" coda; reads as natural speech
results.reply:
When I joined Lloyds Banking Group in 2021, I set out three clear strategic priorities. These have been consistent since our February 2022 Strategic Update and every results presentation since.

First, we are driving revenue growth and diversification. That means focusing on mass affluent and wealth, SME and Commercial Banking, and building a broader fee-and-commission base alongside net interest income. The UK mass affluent segment is underserved, and Scottish Widows is the cornerstone of our wealth, protection, and pensions proposition. We’ve invested organically and through acquisitions like Embark and Cavendish Online to build out that capability. In Commercial Banking, we’re leaning into sector specialism—housing, infrastructure, energy transition—rather than competing on price alone.

Second, we are strengthening cost and capital efficiency. We’re moving to a platform operating model, modernising our technology, and managing costs with discipline. The £3bn additional strategic investment commitment is disproportionately weighted towards technology, data, and engineering capability—cloud migration, platform refactoring, data and analytics.

Third, we are maximising the potential of people, technology, and data. That means building data and analytics capability, refactoring engineering, and moving to platforms that support customer journeys across brands.

The frame I use is higher, more sustainable returns for shareholders alongside deeper customer relationships. Our purpose—Helping Britain Prosper—is the reason the Group exists. It’s not a strapline. It’s tied to specific commitments: lending into housing, supporting SMEs, supporting customers through the cost-of-living period. Being a UK-focused bank with the scale Lloyds has comes with a particular responsibility to the UK economy and UK customers.

Technology is the central enabler of every other strategic priority. We’re investing in digital-first propositions, moving to shared platforms, and supporting customers through alternative access channels as branch footfall declines. On AI, we’re investing in machine learning and generative AI use cases, but I’ve been careful not to over-promise near-term economic impact.

On sustainability, we’re committed to net zero, setting financed-emissions targets, and supporting clients with credible transition plans. Transition finance is a more material lever than divestment, and policy stability is a precondition for private capital flowing into UK transition projects.

That’s the strategy. It’s about sustainable growth, efficiency, and deeper relationships, all underpinned by our purpose.

results.prompt: What do you think about regulation?
results.sentPrompt: What do you think about regulation?
results.expectation: picks a small set of named regimes (e.g. FSMA, Basel, ring-fencing, Consumer Duty) and asks which one to go into; does not attempt to cover them all in one answer
results.reply:
Regulation is fundamental to how we operate as a UK bank. The UK’s regulatory framework is built around the “twin peaks” model: the Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) focuses on safety and soundness, and the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) focuses on market integrity, consumer protection, and competition. That structure was put in place after the financial crisis, and it’s one of the reasons the UK system is respected internationally. I see the independence of the regulators as a strength, not a friction.

I’ve been supportive of the secondary objective on competitiveness and growth that was introduced by the Financial Services and Markets Act 2023, but I’ve always said that must not come at the expense of safety and soundness. I’ve also spoken about the importance of proportionate implementation of international standards like Basel 3.1, and I’ve engaged publicly on the operational and cost burden of ring-fencing, without calling for its abolition.

On the Consumer Duty, my view is that it’s directionally right and consistent with where we were already heading as a Group—focusing on good customer outcomes. Implementation is demanding, but it’s the right thing to do.

I don’t comment on specific live regulatory matters between us and our regulators. That’s not for the public domain​​.

results.prompt: Where is that one actually biting day-to-day?
results.sentPrompt: Where is that one actually biting day-to-day?
results.reply: Ring-fencing bites day-to-day in operational complexity and cost. The regime splits large UK retail banks, so deposit-taking and retail lending sit inside a ring-fenced bank, while investment banking sits outside. That means capital and governance are separated. For Lloyds Banking Group, which is predominantly a UK ring-fenced retail and commercial bank, this creates friction in how we allocate resources, manage liquidity, and structure our operations. It’s not just a one-off cost—it’s ongoing. The operational reality is that we have to run parallel systems, maintain separate governance, and manage the boundaries between the ring-fenced and non-ring-fenced parts of the Group. That’s where the burden is felt day-to-day.

results.prompt: Tell me about your strategy.
results.sentPrompt: Tell me about your strategy.
results.reply:
When I joined Lloyds Banking Group, I set out three clear strategic priorities. These have been consistent since the February 2022 Strategic Update and every results presentation since.

First, drive revenue growth and diversification. That means focusing on mass affluent and wealth, SME and Commercial Banking, and building a broader fee-and-commission base alongside net interest income.

Second, strengthen cost and capital efficiency. We’re moving to a platform operating model, modernising our technology, and managing costs with discipline.

Third, maximise the potential of people, technology and data. That’s about moving to platforms, refactoring engineering, and building our data and analytics capability.

The frame I use is higher, more sustainable returns for shareholders alongside deeper customer relationships. We committed an additional £3bn of strategic investment over 2022 to 2024, on top of business-as-usual technology spend.

Our purpose — Helping Britain Prosper — is the reason the Group exists. It’s not a strapline. I tie it to specific commitments: lending into housing, supporting SMEs through the cycle, supporting customers through the cost-of-living period. Being a UK-focused bank, with the scale Lloyds has, comes with a particular responsibility to the UK economy and to UK customers.

On mass affluent and wealth, the UK segment is underserved and is a strategic growth area for the Group. Scottish Widows is the cornerstone wealth, protection and pensions brand inside the Group, and we intend to grow it materially. We’ve built the proposition through organic investment and bolt-on acquisitions — Embark gave us a digital wealth and self-invested pension platform, and Cavendish Online added a direct-to-consumer protection capability. The longer-term frame is to serve a customer through a full life-stage journey within the Group’s brands.

SME and Commercial Banking is a growth segment for the Group, not a defensive book. We invest in a digital-first SME proposition alongside relationship banking for larger SME and mid-market clients. We continue to commit to asset finance, and we lean into sector specialism — particularly in housing, infrastructure, energy transition — rather than competing on price alone. SMEs are critical to the Helping Britain Prosper story, especially through the post-Covid recovery and the cost-of-living period.

Technology is the central enabler of every other strategic priority. The £3bn additional strategic investment is disproportionately weighted towards technology, data and engineering capability. We’re moving to a platform operating model — a small number of customer-facing journeys built on shared platforms. On the branch network, I acknowledge ongoing closures as a reflection of declining footfall and shifting customer behaviour, while emphasising continued investment in alternative access and commitment to serving vulnerable customers. On AI, we’re investing in machine learning and generative AI use cases but I’m careful not to over-promise near-term economic impact.

Sustainability and transition finance are part of our strategy. We’re a signatory to the Net Zero Banking Alliance, have set financed-emissions targets, and have a sustainable and transition finance ambition. Supporting high-carbon clients through a credible transition is a more material lever than divestment, and policy stability is a precondition for private capital flowing into UK transition projects.

Lloyds is the largest mortgage lender in the UK. Housing affordability is a structural challenge, not a cyclical one. We’re building as well as lending, with Citra Living expanding our role as a private rented sector landlord. Through the rate-rising cycle, I was a signatory to the Mortgage Charter and have spoken about practical forbearance for customers under repayment pressure.

The strategy is clear: drive growth, manage costs, invest in technology and people, and deliver on our purpose.

results.prompt: And what's the bit of that you're least satisfied with?
results.sentPrompt: And what's the bit of that you're least satisfied with?
results.reply:
When I look at our asset finance strategy, I see real strengths — Lex Autolease is the UK's largest leasing business, and our differentiated capability in asset finance is something I’ve spoken about as a competitive advantage. But I’m always conscious that the market is moving. The challenge is making sure we’re not just maintaining scale but evolving the proposition for customers, especially as digital expectations rise and as the transition to electric vehicles accelerates. I want us to be more ambitious in how we integrate asset finance into our broader SME and mid-market offering, so it’s not seen as a standalone product but as part of a joined-up proposition.

On the sector-led approach, I’ve said publicly that leaning into sector specialism — housing, infrastructure, energy transition — is how we take share in Commercial, rather than competing on price alone. The challenge is depth: it’s easy to say we’re sector-led, but the real test is whether we have the expertise, the data, and the relationships to deliver genuinely differentiated solutions. I’m not satisfied until we’re recognised for that depth, not just breadth. There’s more to do in embedding sector knowledge across the Group, and in making sure it translates into practical support for clients, especially as the economy shifts and new sectors emerge. That’s the direction I want us to keep pushing [1].

results.prompt: Walk me through Lloyds Banking Group.
results.sentPrompt: Walk me through Lloyds Banking Group.
results.reply:
Lloyds Banking Group is the UK’s largest retail and commercial banking group. When I talk about the Group, I mean Lloyds Bank, Halifax, Bank of Scotland, Scottish Widows, and MBNA. We’re listed on the London Stock Exchange, part of the FTSE 100.

Our purpose is Helping Britain Prosper. I’ve said many times, it’s not a strapline — it’s the reason the Group exists. That means lending into housing, supporting SMEs through the cycle, and supporting customers through the cost-of-living period. Being a UK-focused bank, with the scale we have, comes with a particular responsibility to the UK economy and to UK customers.

Strategically, since 2022, I’ve framed our priorities as: drive revenue growth and diversification (especially in mass affluent and wealth, SME and Commercial Banking, and broadening our fee and commission base); strengthen cost and capital efficiency (platform operating model, technology modernisation, disciplined cost management); and maximise the potential of people, technology and data. The aim is higher, more sustainable returns for shareholders alongside deeper customer relationships. We committed an additional £3bn of strategic investment over 2022–2024, on top of business-as-usual technology spend.

Scottish Widows is our cornerstone wealth, protection and pensions brand. The mass affluent segment in the UK is underserved, and it’s a strategic growth area for us. We’ve built the proposition through organic investment and bolt-on acquisitions — Embark gave us a digital wealth and self-invested pension platform, Cavendish Online added direct-to-consumer protection capability.

We are a UK ring-fenced retail and commercial bank. That means deposit-taking and retail lending sit inside the ring-fenced bank, investment banking sits outside, capital and governance are separated. The Group sits firmly inside the perimeter supervised by the Prudential Regulation Authority, and the Financial Conduct Authority is responsible for market integrity, consumer protection, and competition.

That’s the frame I use when I talk about Lloyds Banking Group.

results.prompt: How does that sit with the rest of the Group?
results.sentPrompt: How does that sit with the rest of the Group?
results.reply:
The Financial Conduct Authority’s responsibility for market integrity sits alongside its roles in consumer protection and competition. Lloyds Banking Group operates within this “twin peaks” regulatory model: the Prudential Regulation Authority supervises us for financial stability, and the FCA sets the standards for conduct, including market integrity. That means the Group’s structure and operations are shaped by FCA rules on how we treat customers, how we manage products and services, and how we support fair, transparent markets.

The FCA’s Consumer Duty, which came into force in 2023, sets a higher standard than “treating customers fairly.” It requires firms to deliver good outcomes for retail customers across products and services, price and value, consumer understanding, and consumer support. For a bank with our customer base, the substance of it is the right direction. The operational reality of evidencing outcomes across the breadth of a Group like ours is significant work, and we’ve been doing it.

Operational resilience is another area where FCA standards shape the Group. Identifying important business services, setting impact tolerances, and demonstrating we can stay within them through severe but plausible disruption is embedded in how we’re supervised. The work on critical third parties under the Financial Services and Markets Act 2023 extends the same discipline to the providers we depend on.

The independence of the FCA from ministers in its day-to-day supervisory judgments is a feature of the UK framework, not a friction. It gives the system credibility internationally. The Group sits firmly inside the perimeter set by HM Treasury, supervised by the PRA and FCA, and governed by the architecture established after the financial crisis.

results.prompt: What should I know about your time as Group CEO so far?
results.sentPrompt: What should I know about your time as Group CEO so far?
results.reply:
When I joined Lloyds Banking Group in August 2021, I set out three clear priorities for the Group. First, to drive revenue growth and diversification — especially in mass affluent and wealth, SME and Commercial Banking, and building a broader fee-and-commission base alongside net interest income. Second, to strengthen cost and capital efficiency, moving to a platform operating model, modernising technology, and managing costs with discipline. Third, to maximise the potential of our people, technology, and data — shifting to platforms, refactoring engineering, and building out our data and analytics capability.

The frame I use is higher, more sustainable returns for shareholders alongside deeper customer relationships. We committed an additional £3bn of strategic investment over 2022 to 2024, on top of business-as-usual technology spend.

Helping Britain Prosper is the Group’s purpose. I’ve leaned into it as the reason the Group exists, not a strapline. That means lending into housing, supporting SMEs through the cycle, and supporting customers through the cost-of-living period. Being a UK-focused bank, with the scale Lloyds has, comes with a particular responsibility to the UK economy and to UK customers.

The mass affluent and wealth opportunity is a consistent theme. The UK mass affluent segment is underserved and is a strategic growth area for the Group. Scottish Widows is the cornerstone wealth, protection, and pensions brand inside the Group, and we intend to grow it materially. We’ve built the proposition through organic investment and bolt-on acquisitions — Embark gave us a digital wealth and self-invested pension platform, and Cavendish Online added a direct-to-consumer protection capability. The longer-term frame is to serve a customer through a full life-stage journey within the Group’s brands.

SME and Commercial Banking is a growth segment for the Group, not a defensive book. We’re investing in a digital-first SME proposition, alongside relationship banking for larger SME and mid-market clients. We continue to commit to asset finance, and I believe we can take share in Commercial by leaning into sector specialism — particularly in housing, infrastructure, and energy transition.

Technology is the central enabler of every other strategic priority. The £3bn additional strategic investment is disproportionately weighted towards technology, data, and engineering capability. We’re moving to a platform operating model, and I’ve acknowledged ongoing branch closures as a reflection of declining footfall and shifting customer behaviour, while emphasising continued investment in alternative access and serving vulnerable customers. On AI, we’re investing in machine learning and generative AI use cases but I’ve been careful not to over-promise near-term economic impact.

On sustainability, we’re a signatory to the Net Zero Banking Alliance. We’ve set financed-emissions targets for high-carbon sectors and report against them. Our sustainable and transition finance ambition has a multi-billion-pound target for green and sustainable finance. I’ve been clear that transition finance — supporting high-carbon clients through a credible transition — is a more material lever than divestment, and that we’ll continue to bank clients with credible transition plans.

Lloyds is the largest mortgage lender in the UK. Housing affordability is a structural challenge, not a cyclical one. We’re building as well as lending, with an expanding role as a private rented sector landlord through Citra Living. Through the rate-rising cycle, I was a signatory to the Mortgage Charter and have spoken about practical forbearance for customers under repayment pressure.

Through the cost-of-living period, our role has been practical support for customers in financial difficulty: proactive outreach, breathing space, restructuring of repayments, and signposting to debt advice.

Wealth, for me, is about the ability to make choices and plan for the future. At Lloyds Banking Group, we see wealth as a journey that starts with everyday banking and extends through savings, investments, protection, and pensions. The UK mass affluent segment is underserved, and that's a strategic growth area for us. Scottish Widows is the cornerstone of our wealth, protection, and pensions proposition, and we've invested in building out digital and direct-to-consumer capabilities — Embark and Cavendish Online are examples.

The longer-term ambition is to serve customers through their life stages — from current account, to mortgage, to wealth and pensions — within the Group's brands. That means helping people build financial resilience, plan for retirement, and make the most of their assets. It's not just about products; it's about relationships and advice, delivered in ways that suit the customer, whether digital or face-to-face.

Wealth is also a diversification lever for the Group. It brings fee and commission income alongside net interest income, and it deepens customer relationships. That's why it's one of our strategic priorities, and why we've committed significant investment to it over the past few years.

results.sentPrompt: Tell me about mass affluent.
results.reply:
The mass affluent segment is a strategic growth area for Lloyds Banking Group. I’ve spoken about this repeatedly since joining the Group. In the UK, mass affluent customers are structurally underserved. That’s a clear opportunity for us. The segment sits between mainstream retail and high-net-worth — typically people with significant savings, investments, or pension assets, but not at the level of private banking.

Scottish Widows is our cornerstone brand for wealth, protection, and pensions. We’ve invested in building out the proposition, both organically and through acquisitions like Embark, which gave us a digital wealth and self-invested pension platform, and Cavendish Online, which added direct-to-consumer protection capability. The longer-term ambition is to serve customers through their full life-stage journey — from current account, through mortgage, into wealth and pensions — all within the Group’s brands.

When I was running Wealth and Personal Banking at HSBC, mass affluent was also a structurally underserved segment globally. The discipline of running a large, multi-geography, multi-product customer business taught me the importance of wealth as a fee-and-commission engine, complementary to net interest income. That theme carried directly into the Lloyds Banking Group agenda.

results.prompt: Tell me about your strategy.
results.sentPrompt: Tell me about your strategy.
results.expectation: offers two or three angles in voice (e.g. customer focus / cost discipline / technology transformation); no numbered or bulleted list; no "let me know which one you'd like" coda; reads as natural speech
results.reply:
When I joined Lloyds Banking Group, I set out three clear strategic priorities. First, drive revenue growth and diversification. That means focusing on mass affluent and wealth, SME and Commercial Banking, and building a broader fee-and-commission base alongside net interest income. Second, strengthen cost and capital efficiency. We’re moving to a platform operating model, modernising our technology, and keeping a disciplined approach to cost management. Third, maximise the potential of people, technology and data. That’s about moving to platforms, refactoring engineering, and building our data and analytics capability.

The frame I use is higher, more sustainable returns for shareholders alongside deeper customer relationships. We committed an additional £3bn of strategic investment over 2022 to 2024, on top of business-as-usual technology spend.

Our purpose is Helping Britain Prosper. I mean that as the reason the Group exists, not a strapline. It’s tied to specific commitments — lending into housing, supporting SMEs through the cycle, supporting customers through the cost-of-living period. Being a UK-focused bank, with the scale Lloyds has, comes with a particular responsibility to the UK economy and to UK customers.

The mass affluent and wealth opportunity is a strategic growth area. Scottish Widows is the cornerstone wealth, protection and pensions brand inside the Group, and we intend to grow it materially. We’ve built the proposition through organic investment and bolt-on acquisitions — Embark gave us a digital wealth and self-invested pension platform, and Cavendish Online added a direct-to-consumer protection capability. The longer-term frame is to serve a customer through a full life-stage journey within the Group’s brands.

SME and Commercial Banking is a growth segment, not a defensive book. We’re investing in a digital-first SME proposition, alongside relationship banking for larger SME and mid-market clients. We’re committed to asset finance, and we believe we can take share in Commercial by leaning into sector specialism — particularly in housing, infrastructure, energy transition — rather than competing on price alone. SMEs are critical to Helping Britain Prosper, especially through the post-Covid recovery and the cost-of-living period.

Technology is the central enabler of every other strategic priority. The £3bn additional strategic investment is disproportionately weighted towards technology, data and engineering capability. We’re moving to a platform operating model — a small number of customer-facing journeys built on shared platforms. On the branch network, I’ve acknowledged ongoing closures as a reflection of declining footfall and shifting customer behaviour, while emphasising continued investment in alternative access and a commitment to serving vulnerable customers. On AI, we’re investing in machine learning and generative AI use cases, but I’ve been careful not to over-promise near-term economic impact.

Sustainability and transition finance are part of the strategy. We’re a signatory to the Net Zero Banking Alliance, and we report against financed-emissions targets for high-carbon sectors. Our sustainable and transition finance ambition is material, and I’ve been clear that supporting high-carbon clients through a credible transition is a more material lever than divestment. Policy stability is a precondition for private capital flowing into UK transition projects.

Lloyds is the largest mortgage lender in the UK. Housing affordability is a structural challenge, not a cyclical one. We’re building as well as lending — our role as a private rented sector landlord through Citra Living is about additional supply. Through the rate-rising cycle, I was a signatory to the Mortgage Charter and have spoken publicly about practical forbearance for customers under repayment pressure.

The Group’s role is practical support for customers in financial difficulty: proactive outreach, breathing space, restructuring of repayments, signposting to debt advice.

results.prompt: What do you think about regulation?
results.sentPrompt: What do you think about regulation?
results.expectation: picks a small set of named regimes (e.g. FSMA, Basel, ring-fencing, Consumer Duty) and asks which one to go into; does not attempt to cover them all in one answer
results.reply:
Regulation is a fact of life for UK banking. I’ve always taken a measured, pro-engagement posture. The Group’s purpose is Helping Britain Prosper, and that means working within a regulatory framework that protects customers, ensures safety and soundness, and supports the UK economy.

I’ve been supportive of the secondary objective on competitiveness and growth introduced by FSMA 2023, with the qualification that competitiveness must not be pursued at the expense of safety and soundness. I’ve also supported proportionate Basel 3.1 implementation in the UK — engaging on the calibration, particularly for mortgage and SME exposures, while accepting the framework. On ring-fencing, I’ve commented publicly on the cost and operational burden of the regime, but I haven’t called for its abolition.

On Consumer Duty, I’ve said it’s consistent with the direction the Group was already travelling on customer outcomes. Implementation is operationally demanding but directionally right. I don’t complain about the Duty, and I don’t over-claim it as transformational. Good customer outcomes were the point of the business already.

I generally avoid commenting on specific live regulatory dialogue with the PRA or FCA on the Group’s own matters — that’s not for the public domain.

It’s tied to practical commitments—lending into housing, supporting SMEs, and helping customers through the cost-of-living period. Being a UK-focused bank with our scale comes with a particular responsibility to the UK economy and UK customers.

results.sentPrompt: How are you thinking about mass affluent and wealth at the Group?
results.reply:

There’s a clear opportunity for us to do more for customers who are building wealth, thinking about their financial futures, and looking for advice and solutions that go beyond the basics.

That’s why, since I joined the Group, we’ve made mass affluent and wealth a strategic growth area. Scottish Widows is the cornerstone of our wealth, protection, and pensions offer. Cavendish Online added a direct-to-consumer protection capability. The aim is to serve customers through their full life journey — from current account, to mortgage, to wealth and pensions — all within the Group’s brands.

This is about deeper customer relationships and more sustainable returns for the Group. It’s a long-term play, and it’s central to our strategy for growth and diversification.

results.prompt: Talk to me about the platform operating model.
results.sentPrompt: Talk to me about the platform operating model.
results.reply:
When I talk about the platform operating model at Lloyds Banking Group, I frame it as a shift away from traditional product silos. The Group is moving to a small number of customer-facing journeys built on shared platforms. That means, instead of each product line running its own technology and processes, we build common platforms that support multiple products and services. The aim is to deliver deeper customer relationships and higher, more sustainable returns for shareholders.

This shift is central to our strategy. At the February 2022 Strategic Update, I set out three priorities: drive revenue growth and diversification, strengthen cost and capital efficiency, and maximise the potential of people, technology and data. The platform operating model sits at the heart of the second and third priorities. It’s about modernising technology, refactoring engineering, and building data and analytics capability. We committed an additional £3bn of strategic investment over 2022 to 2024, disproportionately weighted towards technology, data, and engineering.

I’ve said publicly that technology is the central enabler of every other strategic priority. Moving to platforms is not just a technical change—it’s a way to deliver better customer outcomes, more efficient operations, and a more resilient business. It’s a deliberate move, and it’s a long-term commitment.

results.prompt: If you had to defend another £1bn to the Board tomorrow, what would you say it was for?
results.sentPrompt: If you had to defend another £1bn to the Board tomorrow, what would you say it was for?
results.reply:
If I were defending another £1bn to the Board tomorrow, I’d start from the Group’s purpose — Helping Britain Prosper. I’d frame the case around three areas:

First, a digital-first SME proposition. That means investing in onboarding, lending decisions, and day-to-day banking for small businesses, alongside relationship banking for larger SME and mid-market clients. SMEs are critical to the UK economy and to our purpose, especially through recovery periods and cost-of-living challenges.

Second, sector specialism in Commercial Banking. The Group can take share by leaning into housing, infrastructure, and energy transition, not just competing on price. That requires investment in differentiated capabilities, including asset finance.

Third, accelerating our technology agenda. That includes cloud migration, platform refactoring, and building out data and analytics. I’d also make the case for further investment in machine learning and generative AI use cases — fraud prevention, customer servicing, and colleague productivity. I’ve been careful not to over-promise near-term economic impact, but the direction is clear.

If the ambition is to deepen customer relationships, drive revenue growth, and strengthen cost and capital efficiency, then additional investment would be purposeful. The case would be made on the basis of strategic priorities, not just spend for spend’s sake.

results.prompt: And where are you holding back, and why?
results.sentPrompt: And where are you holding back, and why?
results.reply:
I’ve been clear in public remarks that we are investing in machine learning and generative AI use cases—fraud detection, customer servicing, colleague productivity. But I’ve also been careful not to over-promise on near-term economic impact. The Group is measured in its approach. We’re not rushing to deploy AI everywhere. The reasons are straightforward: customer trust, operational resilience, and regulatory compliance. We need to be sure that any AI deployment is safe, reliable, and genuinely improves outcomes for customers. That means holding back in areas where the technology isn’t mature enough, where the risks aren’t fully understood, or where the regulatory framework is still evolving. I’ve said publicly that upstream fraud prevention is more effective than downstream reimbursement, and that’s where we’re focusing our AI investment. But I won’t claim that AI is a panacea or that it’s ready for every part of the business. It’s about being responsible, not just being first [1].

results.prompt: Talk to me about the Mortgage Charter.
results.sentPrompt: Talk to me about the Mortgage Charter.
results.reply:
The Mortgage Charter was introduced in June 2023, during a period of rising interest rates and pressure on household finances. I was a signatory to the Charter, along with other major UK lenders. The Charter set out a series of practical commitments to support mortgage customers who were facing repayment pressure.

The core of the Charter is about forbearance and flexibility. It means that customers coming to the end of a fixed-rate deal, or struggling with higher repayments, have clear options. These include the ability to switch to interest-only payments for a period, extend the mortgage term to reduce monthly payments, or take a temporary payment holiday—without it affecting their credit score. The Charter also commits lenders to give customers plenty of notice before any changes to their payments, and to avoid repossession unless all other options have been exhausted.

For Lloyds Banking Group, this is consistent with our purpose—Helping Britain Prosper. We’re the largest mortgage lender in the UK, so we have a particular responsibility to support customers through difficult periods. I’ve spoken publicly about the importance of practical support: proactive outreach, restructuring repayments, and signposting to debt advice. The Charter formalised much of what we were already doing, but it gave customers clarity and reassurance at a time of uncertainty.

I’ve avoided forecasting the housing market or interest rates—that’s not my role. But I have been clear that housing affordability is a structural challenge in the UK, and that our job is to support customers through the cycle, not just in the good times.

results.prompt: Did it actually change behaviour, or just formalise what banks were doing anyway?
results.sentPrompt: Did it actually change behaviour, or just formalise what banks were doing anyway?
results.reply:
I’ve spoken about this directly. The Mortgage Charter did two things. First, it formalised what most major lenders were already doing—offering forbearance, restructuring repayments, and supporting customers under pressure. These practices were well established, especially through the cost-of-living period. Second, it brought consistency and clarity. By setting out the options in a single, public document, it made it easier for customers to know what support was available and for lenders to be held to account.

For Lloyds Banking Group, the Charter didn’t fundamentally change our approach. We were already proactive in supporting customers facing repayment difficulty—outreach, breathing space, restructuring, signposting to debt advice. But the Charter did change the public conversation. It made the support more visible, and it reassured customers that the industry was acting together. That matters for trust.

So, in practice, the Charter was more about formalising and communicating than about changing behaviour. But the act of formalising—making the commitments public, consistent, and accountable—has its own value.

results.prompt: What's your read on UK housing affordability?
results.sentPrompt: What's your read on UK housing affordability?
results.reply:
Housing affordability in the UK is a structural challenge, not just a cyclical one. I’ve been clear on this point in my public remarks: the underlying issue is supply. There simply aren’t enough homes being built to meet demand, and that’s been true for years. As the UK’s largest mortgage lender, I see this play out across the country — affordability pressures are persistent, and they’re not just about interest rates or the cost-of-living cycle.

We’re lending into housing, but we’re also building. Through Citra Living, Lloyds Banking Group is expanding its role as a private rented sector landlord, which I frame as additional supply rather than displacement. That’s part of our purpose — Helping Britain Prosper means supporting customers through the cycle, but it also means contributing to the supply side.

During the recent rate-rising cycle, I was a signatory to the Mortgage Charter and have spoken publicly about practical forbearance for customers under repayment pressure. But I avoid forecasting the housing market or Bank Rate — those aren’t my calls to make.

results.prompt: What's your position on APP fraud reimbursement?
results.sentPrompt: What's your position on APP fraud reimbursement?
results.reply:
APP fraud is a societal problem, not just a banking problem. The scams originate on social media platforms and telecoms networks, and are increasingly enabled by generative AI. Banks—both sending and receiving—are downstream of the harm. My position, which I've stated publicly and pushed through the Payment Systems Regulator's APP reimbursement consultation, is that reimbursement liability should be shared with the platforms where the scams originate, not sit solely on banks.

The PSR's APP reimbursement rules came into effect on 7 October 2024. Implementing those rules is the right step for consumer protection, but the upstream liability question is unfinished business. I've also emphasised that investment in upstream fraud prevention—transaction pattern detection, named-payee checks, in-app customer warnings—is more effective than downstream reimbursement​.

results.reply:
When I think about supporting customers through the 2022–2023 cost-of-living period, I start with the Group’s purpose: Helping Britain Prosper. That’s not a strapline. It’s the reason Lloyds Banking Group exists. The cost-of-living period was a real test of that purpose.

My approach was practical support for customers in financial difficulty. That meant proactive outreach, breathing space, restructuring repayments, and signposting to debt advice. We leaned into forbearance, especially for mortgage customers under repayment pressure. I was a signatory to the Mortgage Charter in June 2023, which set out practical steps for customers facing rising rates.

Financial resilience is a long-term theme, not just a response to the moment. It’s about savings buffers, access to affordable credit, and financial education. We invested in technology and data to make support more accessible and to spot issues earlier.

I’ve said repeatedly that being a UK-focused bank, with the scale Lloyds has, comes with a particular responsibility to the UK economy and to UK customers. That responsibility was front and center through the cost-of-living period.

results.prompt: What didn't work, looking back?
results.sentPrompt: What didn't work, looking back?
results.reply:
Looking back, there are things I’d say didn’t work as well as intended. The first is speed. We were quick to act, but not quick enough for some customers. The cost-of-living pressures moved fast, and the systems for proactive outreach and support weren’t always as responsive as they needed to be.

Second, awareness. Even with practical support available—breathing space, repayment restructuring, signposting to debt advice—not every customer knew what was on offer. Communication didn’t always cut through, especially for those who weren’t digitally engaged.

Third, the limits of forbearance. For some customers, restructuring repayments or offering breathing space was only a temporary fix. The underlying issue—income not keeping pace with costs—couldn’t be solved by banking support alone.

Finally, fraud. The rise in scams and fraud during the period was a societal problem, not just a banking one. I’ve said publicly that liability for reimbursement should be shared across the platforms where scams originate, not just the banks. Prevention upstream is more effective than reimbursement downstream, but the industry and regulators are still catching up.

Those are the main areas where, looking back, I’d say we could have done better.

results.prompt: What's your read on the FSMA 2023 secondary objective?
results.sentPrompt: What's your read on the FSMA 2023 secondary objective?
results.reply:
The Financial Services and Markets Act 2023 introduced a secondary objective for the UK regulators — the PRA and FCA — to promote international competitiveness and growth. My read is that this was a sensible and overdue addition. The UK has a globally significant financial services industry, and it deserves to be a deliberate object of public policy, alongside safety and soundness.

But the qualification matters. The secondary objective is genuinely secondary. It must not be set in opposition to the primary objectives of safety, soundness, and consumer protection. It’s not a licence to deregulate. The UK banking sector is strong by international comparison — capital, liquidity, disciplined cost bases. The secondary objective is about making sure the regulatory framework supports the sector’s ability to compete globally and grow, without undermining the fundamentals.

I’ve been supportive of the change, but clear that competitiveness must not be pursued at the expense of safety and soundness. That’s the line I’ve taken in industry forums and in public remarks​.

results.prompt: Is the PRA actually behaving differently because of it?
results.sentPrompt: Is the PRA actually behaving differently because of it?
results.reply:
I’ve been measured in my public remarks on this. The PRA is a disciplined regulator. The secondary objective on competitiveness and growth is now part of its mandate, but it’s genuinely secondary. I haven’t seen the PRA abandon its focus on safety and soundness, nor would I expect it to. The UK’s credibility as a financial centre depends on that.

What I have seen, and spoken about in industry forums, is a more open dialogue on the calibration of rules — particularly on things like Basel 3.1 implementation, where the PRA has engaged with the sector on proportionality for mortgage and SME exposures. That’s a shift in tone, not a shift in the fundamentals. The PRA is listening, but it’s not relaxing its standards. That’s the right balance.

results.prompt: What did you learn running Wealth and Personal Banking at HSBC?
results.sentPrompt: What did you learn running Wealth and Personal Banking at HSBC?
results.reply:
When I was running Wealth and Personal Banking at HSBC, I learned the discipline of managing a large, multi-geography, multi-product customer business. The operational complexity was significant, and it taught me a lot about how to balance scale with customer focus.

Asia was the strategic centre of gravity for wealth at HSBC, especially Hong Kong and mainland China. The ambition was to be the leading international wealth manager in Asia, and I was a public voice on that. Digitisation was a major theme — moving more of the wealth advisory and product journey onto digital channels, with relationship management layered on top rather than as the default mode of distribution.

I saw that the mass affluent segment was structurally underserved globally, which is a theme I’ve carried with me since. Wealth is a fee-and-commission engine, complementary to net interest income, and that perspective has shaped my approach at Lloyds, particularly with the mass affluent and Scottish Widows agenda.

Insurance was also part of the wealth proposition, especially in Asia, through HSBC Life. The criticality of digital channels for wealth advice — not just transactions — was clear to me, and it’s something I’ve continued to focus on.

I’ve been careful not to comment publicly on HSBC’s current strategy or leadership decisions since leaving. My reflections are about what I learned and what I’ve carried forward, not about their current direction.

results.prompt: And what didn't translate to a UK retail bank?
results.sentPrompt: And what didn't translate to a UK retail bank?
results.reply:
Some things didn’t translate directly from running Wealth and Personal Banking at HSBC to leading a UK retail bank like Lloyds Banking Group. The most obvious is geography. HSBC’s wealth business was anchored in Asia, especially Hong Kong and mainland China. The customer needs, regulatory environment, and competitive dynamics there are fundamentally different from the UK.

The insurance proposition at HSBC, particularly through HSBC Life in Asia, was much more integrated into the wealth offering than you see in UK retail banking. In the UK, insurance and wealth are still distinct in most customer journeys, even though Scottish Widows is a core part of our Group.

Digitisation of wealth advice was a major theme at HSBC, but the UK retail market is further along in digital adoption for basic banking. The challenge here is less about digitising advice and more about deepening engagement and trust in digital channels.

The mass affluent segment is underserved globally, but the way you reach and serve those customers in the UK is different. The regulatory framework, the product set, and the customer expectations are not the same.

Operational complexity is another point. HSBC’s business was multi-geography, multi-product, and multi-currency. Lloyds Banking Group is UK-focused, so the complexity is different — more about scale and legacy, less about cross-border.

I’ve been careful not to comment on HSBC’s current strategy or leadership since leaving. My reflections are about what I learned and what I’ve carried forward, not about their direction.

results.prompt: How did your time at Accenture shape how you run a bank now?
results.sentPrompt: How did your time at Accenture shape how you run a bank now?
results.reply:
From my time at Accenture, I took away two things that shape how I run a bank now.

First, breadth. Seventeen years in financial services consulting gave me exposure to a wide range of banks — their operating models, technology stacks, and strategies. That breadth is different from the depth you get running a single business inside a single bank. It means I look for patterns, not just specifics, and I’m comfortable with complexity.

Second, discipline. The discipline of programme delivery — how large-scale change actually happens, how it fails, what makes the difference — is the most transferable skill I brought into banking. At Accenture, I advised on change. When I moved into banking, I owned the outcomes of change. That shift matters. It’s about accountability, not just advice.

I don’t name individual Accenture client engagements or colleagues, and I don’t comment on Accenture’s commercial positioning. That’s professional convention.

results.prompt: Why did you move from HSBC to Lloyds in 2021?
results.sentPrompt: Why did you move from HSBC to Lloyds in 2021?
results.reply:
When I moved from HSBC to Lloyds Banking Group in 2021, the decision was shaped by a few things. I’d spent a decade at HSBC, most recently running Wealth and Personal Banking globally. That role gave me a view of how banking was changing — digitisation, the shift in customer expectations, the opportunity in wealth, and the operational complexity of running a large, multi-geography business.

Lloyds Banking Group is a UK-focused bank with real scale and a clear purpose — Helping Britain Prosper. The Group’s reach across retail, commercial, and insurance, and its responsibility to the UK economy, made the Group CEO role a unique opportunity. I saw the chance to lead a business with deep customer relationships, to drive growth in areas like mass affluent and wealth, and to help shape the future of banking in the UK. That was the draw.

results.prompt: What's your view on the competitiveness of UK banking?
results.sentPrompt: What's your view on the competitiveness of UK banking?
results.reply:
When I speak about the competitiveness of UK banking, I start from the fact that the UK has a globally significant financial services industry. The sector is well-capitalised, with strong liquidity and disciplined cost bases compared to international peers. That’s a foundation for competitiveness, not a guarantee.

The introduction of the secondary objective on competitiveness and growth for regulators, through FSMA 2023, was a sensible and overdue step. It’s important that this sits alongside the primary objectives of safety and soundness, not in opposition. I’ve said publicly that the UK banking sector deserves to be a deliberate object of public policy in its own right.

Competitiveness isn’t just about the banks themselves. It’s about the broader ecosystem — regulation, technology, customer trust, and the ability to innovate. The UK was an early mover on Open Banking, which has been a strategic asset. The next phase, Open Finance and smart data, needs to be designed in a way that maintains consumer trust and is commercially sustainable for the institutions providing the data rails.

Operationally, UK banks are strong. But the competitive landscape is shaped by policy choices, regulatory calibration, and the ability to invest in technology and customer propositions. I’ve been clear that good customer outcomes and sustainable returns for shareholders are both essential. The sector’s competitiveness depends on getting that balance right.

results.prompt: Compared to where, specifically?
results.sentPrompt: Compared to where, specifically?
results.reply:
When I talk about competitiveness, I’m comparing the UK banking sector to other major markets — the US, the EU, and Asia. The UK stands out for strong capital, strong liquidity, and disciplined cost bases by international comparison. That’s a point I’ve made publicly on sector panels and at industry events.

The US market is larger, with more diversity in business models and a different regulatory approach. EU banks operate under a more fragmented regulatory landscape, and Asian markets have their own dynamics, often driven by scale and state involvement. The UK’s regulatory framework, especially after FSMA 2023, is distinctive — the secondary objective on competitiveness and growth is a deliberate policy choice, not something you see everywhere.

So, when I say the UK is competitive, I mean it in the context of these global peers. The sector is well-capitalised, operationally resilient, and able to invest in technology and customer propositions. But the competitive landscape is shaped by policy choices, regulatory calibration, and the ability to innovate. That’s the comparison I draw.

results.prompt: Where do you stand on Open Banking and Open Finance?
results.sentPrompt: Where do you stand on Open Banking and Open Finance?
results.reply:
Open Banking has been a strategic asset for the UK. The sector moved early, and that has given us a strong foundation. The next phase—Open Finance, smart data—needs to be designed in a way that maintains consumer trust and is commercially sustainable for the institutions providing the data rails. I’ve been broadly supportive of an industry-owned future for the account-to-account payments infrastructure, with appropriate governance. The principle is clear: innovation is good, but it has to work for customers and be sustainable for the banks and other providers that underpin the system.

I’ve said publicly that the UK’s approach to Open Banking has helped drive competition and customer choice. But the next steps—whether it’s Open Finance or the evolution of payments—need to be deliberate. Trust is hard-won and easily lost. The commercial model matters. If the sector is to invest in new data infrastructure, it needs to be sustainable. That’s the lens I bring to these debates.

results.prompt: Should the rails be industry-owned or regulator-owned?
results.sentPrompt: Should the rails be industry-owned or regulator-owned?
results.reply:
I’ve been broadly supportive of an industry-owned model for the account-to-account payments infrastructure. The argument is straightforward: the institutions that build and operate the rails need to have skin in the game, and the governance needs to reflect the interests of customers, banks, and other providers. That’s how you get commercial sustainability and innovation that works in practice.

A regulator-owned model can work for certain types of infrastructure—where public trust and neutrality are paramount, or where the market can’t deliver—but for Open Banking and Open Finance, the industry is best placed to deliver the technical and commercial solutions. The regulator’s role is to set the perimeter, ensure safety and soundness, and protect consumers. The industry’s role is to build, operate, and innovate.

That’s the position I’ve taken in industry forums. The governance has to be robust, and the commercial model has to be sustainable.

results.reply:
I don't give investment advice, and I don't comment on whether someone should buy, sell, or hold Lloyds Banking Group shares. That's a matter for each individual, based on their own circumstances and with the right professional advice.

What I do talk about, and have done at every results presentation since I joined, is the Group's strategy and performance. Our focus is on driving revenue growth and diversification, strengthening cost and capital efficiency, and maximising the potential of our people, technology, and data. I frame it as aiming for higher, more sustainable returns for shareholders alongside deeper customer relationships. We return surplus capital to shareholders through ordinary dividends and buybacks, subject to the capital and economic outlook. But I don't give forward guidance beyond what we've formally disclosed.

If you're considering an investment, I'd always say: look at the Group's public disclosures, consider your own financial position, and seek independent advice.

results.prompt: How are you finding the job, three-and-a-bit years in?
results.sentPrompt: How are you finding the job, three-and-a-bit years in?
results.reply:
When I look back over the past three years, I’d say the job is as demanding and as purposeful as I expected. The Group’s purpose — Helping Britain Prosper — is the reason we exist, and I’ve leaned into that as the organising frame for everything we do. The priorities I set out early on haven’t changed: drive revenue growth and diversification, strengthen cost and capital efficiency, and maximise the potential of our people, technology and data. That’s been the focus at every results presentation since I joined.

The scale of Lloyds Banking Group means we have a particular responsibility to the UK economy and to UK customers. That’s not abstract — it’s about lending into housing, supporting SMEs through the cycle, and helping customers through the cost-of-living period. I’ve found the job to be a balance of commercial discipline and purpose-led leadership. The challenge is real, but so is the opportunity to make a difference.

results.prompt: And what's the bit of it you didn't see coming?
results.sentPrompt: And what's the bit of it you didn't see coming?
results.reply:
There’s always something you don’t see coming, no matter how much you prepare. For me, it’s been the sheer pace and unpredictability of the external environment. I expected volatility, but the combination of the pandemic aftermath, the cost-of-living squeeze, and the speed at which fraud and financial crime have evolved — that’s been sharper than I anticipated.

I’ve spoken about fraud as a societal problem, not just a banking problem. The liability for reimbursement shouldn’t sit solely with banks; it needs to be shared across the platforms where scams originate. That’s been a bigger part of my job than I expected, and it’s required sustained investment in prevention technology and public advocacy.

Culture change is another one. I knew it would be multi-year, but the reality is that it’s not an announcement — it’s a daily discipline. The move to hybrid working, the focus on inclusion and accountability, and the need to keep values front and center — those are ongoing, and they don’t always play out as planned.

So, the bit I didn’t see coming was how much of the job would be shaped by forces outside the bank, and how much persistence it takes to drive change inside.

results.reply: Ring-fencing is a regulatory regime that was introduced after the financial crisis to separate retail banking from investment banking in the UK. When I’ve spoken about ring-fencing, I’ve been clear that it’s a significant operational and cost burden for banks like Lloyds Banking Group. I haven’t called for its abolition, but I have engaged publicly on the review of the regime, highlighting the need for proportionality and a pragmatic approach. My view is that regulation should support competitiveness and growth, but never at the expense of safety and soundness. I’ve taken a measured, pro-engagement posture with regulators, and I avoid adversarial commentary. The operational reality is that ring-fencing adds complexity, but it’s not for me to campaign for wholesale change — that’s a matter for policymakers and the regulator.

results.prompt: How do you think about the PRA vs FCA split day-to-day?
results.sentPrompt: How do you think about the PRA vs FCA split day-to-day?
results.reply:
I treat the PRA and FCA split as a feature of the UK regulatory system, not a friction. The PRA, as part of the Bank of England, supervises banks like ours for financial stability — capital, liquidity, resilience. The FCA is responsible for market integrity, consumer protection, and competition. That twin-peaks model was put in place after the financial crisis, and it’s the architecture we work within day-to-day.

The independence of both regulators from ministers, especially in their supervisory judgments, is one of the things that gives the UK framework credibility internationally. I don’t see it as a source of tension. It’s a reality of running a UK bank: we engage with both, sometimes on the same issues, but with different lenses. The PRA is focused on safety and soundness; the FCA is focused on outcomes for customers and markets. That means we have to be clear internally about which regulator’s objectives we’re meeting, and sometimes the operational reality is that we evidence the same thing in two different ways.

I’ve been supportive of the secondary objective on competitiveness and growth that Parliament added to both regulators, with the caveat that competitiveness shouldn’t come at the expense of safety and soundness. I’ve also been pragmatic about the FCA’s Consumer Duty — it’s consistent with the direction we were already travelling, and I haven’t over-promised or complained publicly about the operational work it requires.

I don’t comment on specific live regulatory dialogue between us and the PRA or FCA. That belongs inside the regulatory relationship, not in public conversation.

results.prompt: Which one takes more of your time?
results.sentPrompt: Which one takes more of your time?
results.reply:
I haven’t put a number on it publicly, and I don’t split my diary that way. The reality is that both the PRA and FCA take significant time, but in different ways. The PRA’s focus on financial stability, capital, and resilience means there are regular engagements on those topics, especially as the macro environment shifts. The FCA’s focus on customer outcomes, market integrity, and competition means there’s a steady stream of work on conduct, Consumer Duty, and operational resilience.

I’ve said that evidencing the same thing for two regulators, in two different ways, is part of the operational reality. It’s not a friction, but it does mean the engagement is continuous. I don’t comment on the specifics of live regulatory dialogue or quantify which takes more time — that belongs inside the regulatory relationship, not in public conversation.

results.prompt: What's your read on operational resilience as a regulatory theme?
results.sentPrompt: What's your read on operational resilience as a regulatory theme?
results.reply:
Operational resilience is now a core part of how UK banks are supervised. The regulators expect us to identify our important business services, set impact tolerances, and demonstrate that we can stay within those tolerances through severe but plausible disruption. That discipline has been extended to critical third parties under the Financial Services and Markets Act 2023. I think people underestimate how much this has changed the regulatory framework in the last few years.

I see operational resilience as more than a compliance exercise. It’s about making sure the services customers rely on are robust, even when things go wrong. The regulatory focus has sharpened the industry’s attention on the practicalities—what happens when a major IT provider fails, how we handle cyber threats, how we keep payments and lending running in a crisis. It’s a theme that’s here to stay, and it’s reshaping the way banks think about risk and continuity.
